'''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Konden Einen Shizai Law]] }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[sn:Konden Einen Shizai Law]] [[en:Konden Einen Shizai Law]] [[ja:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]]

revision.comment: 172 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:墾田永年私財法 up to rev 102390797
revision.text:
{{Otheruses||[[レキシ]]の楽曲|レキミ}} '''墾田永年私財法'''（こんでんえいねんしざいほう）は、[[奈良時代]]中期の[[聖武天皇]]の治世に、[[天平]]15年[[5月27日 (旧暦)|5月27日]]（[[743年]][[6月23日]]）に発布された[[勅]]（[[天皇]]の名による命令）で、[[墾田]]（自分で新しく[[開墾]]した田地）の耕作権の永年私財化を認める[[法令]]である。
'''墾田永世私財法'''（こんでんえいせいしざいほう<ref name="Daijisen">{{Cite Kotobank|word=墾田永年私財法|encyclopedia=デジタル大辞泉|access-date=2023年4月16日}}</ref>）、'''墾田永世私有法'''（こんでんえいせいしゆうほう<ref name="Daijisen" />）、'''墾田永代私有令'''（こんでんえいたいしゆうれい<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=墾田永代私有令|encyclopedia=旺文社日本史事典 三訂版|access-date=2023年4月17日}}</ref>）ともいう。[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]発生の基礎となった法令である<ref name="Mypedia" />。
== 原文 == === 類聚三代格 === 墾田永年私財法の原文は『[[類聚三代格]]』巻十五に収録されている。 {{Quotation|勅、墾田拠養老七年格、限満之後依例収穫、由是農夫怠倦開地復荒、自今以後、任為私財無論三世一身、悉咸永年莫取、其国司在任之日、墾田一依前格、但人為開田占地者、先就国申請、然後開之、不得因茲占請百姓有妨之地、若受地之後至于三年、本主不開者、聴他人開墾。
天平十五年五月廿七日 |『[[類聚三代格]]』巻十五 墾田并佃事 }}
現代日本語訳は下記の通り。 {{Quotation| （[[聖武天皇]]が）命令する。聞くところによれば、これまで墾田の取扱いは[[三世一身法]]（養老7年格）に基づき、満期になれば収公し、通例どおり(国有田として他の耕作者へ)授与していた。しかし、そのために(開墾した)農民は意欲を失って怠け、開墾した土地が再び荒れることとなった。今後は、私財とすることを認め、三世一身にかかわらず、全て永年にわたり収公してはならない。在任中の[[国司]]による墾田の扱いは、前の格に準拠せよ。ただし、耕地を開墾してその土地を占有しようとする者は、まず国に申請し、しかる後に開墾せよ。この規定を根拠として公衆に妨げのある土地を占有することはできない。もし許可を受けた者が3年経っても開墾しない場合は、他の者からの開墾の申請を受け付ける。
天平15年5月27日 }}
=== 続日本紀 === 『[[続日本紀]]』巻十五の同日条を見ると、同じく天皇による命令形式である[[詔]]として出され、その中に[[位階]]に応じて私有面積の上限を定めた規定が入っていたことが分かる。
この部分は、[[位階]]に応じて私有面積の上限を定めた規定であるが、この部分が上記の『類聚三代格』では削除されている。
{{Quotation| 乙丑。詔曰。如聞。墾田依養老七年格。限滿之後。依例收授。由是。農夫怠倦。開地復荒。自今以後。任爲私財無論三世一身。咸悉永年莫取。其親王一品及一位五百町。二品及二位四百町。三品四品及三位三百町。四位二百町。五位百町。六位已下八位已上五十町。初位已下至于庶人十町。但郡司者。大領少領三十町。主政主帳十町。若有先給地過多茲限。便即還公。姦作隱欺科罪如法。國司在任之日。墾田一依前格。 |『[[続日本紀]]』巻十五 五月乙丑の条 }}
現代語訳： {{Quotation| ……（その土地の広さは）親王の一品と一位には五百町、二品と二位には四百町、三品・四品と三位には三百町、四位には二百町、五位には百町、六位以下八位以上には五十町、初位以下（無位の）庶人に至るまでは十町（とせよ）。ただし、郡司には、大領・少領には三十町、主政・主帳には十町とせよ。もし以前に与えられた土地で、この限度よりも多いものがあれば、すみやかに公に還せ。不正に土地を所有して隠し欺くものがあれば、罪を科すことは法の如くにする。…… |[[直木孝次郎]]ほか訳注 『続日本紀 2』 平凡社〈[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]〉（{{ISBN2|4-582-80489-6}}）による） }}
== 背景 == [[律令制]]・[[班田収授制]]開始後、8世紀初頭には人口増大に対し、班給すべき[[口分田]]が不足し始め、[[墾田]]への動機付けを強める政策が始まった。
[[養老]]7年（[[723年]]）に出された[[三世一身法]]によって、墾田は孫までの3代の間に私財化が認められたが、収公期限が引き続き定められたため、収公の時期が迫ると荒廃することがあった<ref name="Sekaidaihyakka">{{Cite Kotobank|word=墾田永年私財法|encyclopedia=世界大百科事典 第2版|access-date=2023年4月16日}}</ref>。
また当時の日本は、[[天平の疫病大流行]]（[[天平]]7年（[[735年]]）から同9年（[[737年]]））により大打撃を受けたところでもあった。
その数年後に出された墾田永年私財法は、墾田を永年にわたり私財とすることを可能とし、社会復興策の一面も強かった<ref name=farris69>{{Cite book2|language=en|last=Farris|first=William Wayne|title=Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=1985|page=69|isbn=978-0674690059}}</ref>。
このほか、『[[日本大百科全書]]』では天平15年に[[左大臣]]に昇進した[[橘諸兄]]の政策的意図もあったとしている<ref name="Nipponica">{{Cite Kotobank|word=墾田永年私財法|encyclopedia=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|access-date=2023年4月17日}}</ref>。
== 内容 == [[三世一身法]]にあった収公期限を廃止して、墾田を永年にわたり私財とすることを可能とした<ref name="Nipponica" />。ただし、下記の制限が定められた。 *新規の開墾地で適用され、既墾地では引き続き[[班田収授法]]に準拠する<ref name="Nipponica" />。 *私財とできる面積は[[位階]]により10から500[[町 (単位)|町]]までと定められた<ref name="Nipponica" />。 *[[国司]]の許可を必要とする<ref name="Mypedia">{{Cite Kotobank|word=墾田永年私財法|encyclopedia=百科事典マイペディア|access-date=2023年4月17日}}</ref>。 *国司の許可から3年内に開墾を完了させる<ref name="Mypedia" />（3年内に着手すべきとする説もある<ref name="Obunsha">{{Cite Kotobank|word=墾田永年私財法|encyclopedia=旺文社日本史事典 三訂版|access-date=2023年4月17日}}</ref>）。 *百姓の妨げとならないこと<ref name="Obunsha" />。
== 法の中断 == [[道鏡]]が[[孝謙天皇|称徳天皇]]の後見で[[太政大臣]]禅師に就くと、[[天平神護]]元年[[3月6日 (旧暦)|3月6日]]（[[765年]][[3月31日]]）に、過熱した墾田を止めるために、寺院や農民の小規模な開墾は許すが<ref name="Nipponica" />、墾田私有を原則的に禁止する旨の[[太政官符]]が発布された<ref name="Sandai">『[[類聚三代格]]』巻十五 墾田并佃事 「聴墾田事」の条。</ref>。
しかし称徳天皇が崩御し、[[光仁天皇]]が即位したことで[[道鏡]]が失脚すると、[[宝亀]]3年[[10月14日 (旧暦)|10月14日]]（[[772年]][[11月13日]]）に、墾田私有を原則的に許可するとの太政官符が発布された<ref name="Sandai" />（ただし百姓は苦しませないよう留意が求められた）。
== 影響 == 墾田私有が認められたため、[[豪族]]や社寺が開墾を進めて土地私有に動き、[[荘園 (日本)|荘園制]]成立と[[班田収授法]]崩壊の原因を作った<ref name="Nipponica" /><ref name="Obunsha" />。この動きにより成立した荘園は自墾地系荘園または[[初期荘園|墾田地系荘園]]と呼ばれる<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=自墾地系荘園|encyclopedia=デジタル大辞泉|access-date=2023年4月17日}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連図書 == *{{Cite book|language=ja|author=森田悌|authorlink=森田悌|title=平安時代政治史研究|publisher=吉川弘文館|date=1978年|isbn=4-642-02088-8}}
== 関連項目 == *[[律令制]] *[[三世一身法]] *[[条里制]] *[[加墾禁止令]] *[[ホームステッド法]]
{{デフォルトソート:こんてんえいねんしさいほう}} [[Category:日本の格式]] [[Category:日本の土地制度史]] [[Category:奈良時代の政治]] [[Category:8世紀の法]] [[Category:743年の日本]] [[Category:日本の農業関連法規]] [[Category:聖武天皇]]

{{Quotation| 乙丑。詔曰。如聞。墾田依養老七年格。限滿之後。依例收授。由是。農夫怠倦。開地復荒。自今以後。任爲私財無論三世一身。咸悉永年莫取。其親王一品及一位五百町。二品及二位四百町。三品四品及三位三百町。四位二百町。五位百町。六位已下八位已上五十町。初位已下至于庶人十町。但郡司者。大領少領三十町。主政主帳十町。若有先給地過多茲限。便即還公。姦作隱欺科罪如法。國司在任之日。墾田一依前格。 |『[[続日本紀]]』巻十五 五月乙丑の条 }}
現代語訳： {{Quotation| ……（その土地の広さは）親王の一品と一位には五百町、二品と二位には四百町、三品・四品と三位には三百町、四位には二百町、五位には百町、六位以下八位以上には五十町、初位以下（無位の）庶人に至るまでは十町（とせよ）。ただし、郡司には、大領・少領には三十町、主政・主帳には十町とせよ。もし以前に与えられた土地で、この限度よりも多いものがあれば、すみやかに公に還せ。不正に土地を所有して隠し欺くものがあれば、罪を科すことは法の如くにする。…… |[[直木孝次郎]]ほか訳注 『続日本紀 2』 平凡社〈[[東洋文庫 (平凡社)|東洋文庫]]〉（{{ISBN2|4-582-80489-6}}）による） }}
== 背景 == [[律令制]]・[[班田収授制]]開始後、8世紀初頭には人口増大に対し、班給すべき[[口分田]]が不足し始め、[[墾田]]への動機付けを強める政策が始まった。
[[養老]]7年（[[723年]]）に出された[[三世一身法]]によって、墾田は孫までの3代の間に私財化が認められたが、収公期限が引き続き定められたため、収公の時期が迫ると荒廃することがあった<ref name="Sekaidaihyakka">{{Cite Kotobank|word=墾田永年私財法|encyclopedia=世界大百科事典 第2版|access-date=2023年4月16日}}</ref>。
また当時の日本は、[[天平の疫病大流行]]（[[天平]]7年（[[735年]]）から同9年（[[737年]]））により大打撃を受けたところでもあった。
その数年後に出された墾田永年私財法は、墾田を永年にわたり私財とすることを可能とし、社会復興策の一面も強かった<ref name=farris69>{{Cite book2|language=en|last=Farris|first=William Wayne|title=Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900|publisher=Harvard University Asia Center|year=1985|page=69|isbn=978-0674690059}}</ref>。
このほか、『[[日本大百科全書]]』では天平15年に[[左大臣]]に昇進した[[橘諸兄]]の政策的意図もあったとしている<ref name="Nipponica">{{Cite Kotobank|word=墾田永年私財法|encyclopedia=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|access-date=2023年4月17日}}</ref>。
== 内容 == [[三世一身法]]にあった収公期限を廃止して、墾田を永年にわたり私財とすることを可能とした<ref name="Nipponica" />。ただし、下記の制限が定められた。 *新規の開墾地で適用され、既墾地では引き続き[[班田収授法]]に準拠する<ref name="Nipponica" />。 *私財とできる面積は[[位階]]により10から500[[町 (単位)|町]]までと定められた<ref name="Nipponica" />。 *[[国司]]の許可を必要とする<ref name="Mypedia">{{Cite Kotobank|word=墾田永年私財法|encyclopedia=百科事典マイペディア|access-date=2023年4月17日}}</ref>。 *国司の許可から3年内に開墾を完了させる<ref name="Mypedia" />（3年内に着手すべきとする説もある<ref name="Obunsha">{{Cite Kotobank|word=墾田永年私財法|encyclopedia=旺文社日本史事典 三訂版|access-date=2023年4月17日}}</ref>）。 *百姓の妨げとならないこと<ref name="Obunsha" />。
== 法の中断 == [[道鏡]]が[[孝謙天皇|称徳天皇]]の後見で[[太政大臣]]禅師に就くと、[[天平神護]]元年[[3月6日 (旧暦)|3月6日]]（[[765年]][[3月31日]]）に、過熱した墾田を止めるために、寺院や農民の小規模な開墾は許すが<ref name="Nipponica" />、墾田私有を原則的に禁止する旨の[[太政官符]]が発布された<ref name="Sandai">『[[類聚三代格]]』巻十五 墾田并佃事 「聴墾田事」の条。</ref>。
しかし称徳天皇が崩御し、[[光仁天皇]]が即位したことで[[道鏡]]が失脚すると、[[宝亀]]3年[[10月14日 (旧暦)|10月14日]]（[[772年]][[11月13日]]）に、墾田私有を原則的に許可するとの太政官符が発布された<ref name="Sandai" />（ただし百姓は苦しませないよう留意が求められた）。
== 影響 == 墾田私有が認められたため、[[豪族]]や社寺が開墾を進めて土地私有に動き、[[荘園 (日本)|荘園制]]成立と[[班田収授法]]崩壊の原因を作った<ref name="Nipponica" /><ref name="Obunsha" />。この動きにより成立した荘園は自墾地系荘園または[[初期荘園|墾田地系荘園]]と呼ばれる<ref>{{Cite Kotobank|word=自墾地系荘園|encyclopedia=デジタル大辞泉|access-date=2023年4月17日}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連図書 == *{{Cite book|language=ja|author=森田悌|authorlink=森田悌|title=平安時代政治史研究|publisher=吉川弘文館|date=1978年|isbn=4-642-02088-8}}
== 関連項目 == *[[律令制]] *[[三世一身法]] *[[条里制]] *[[加墾禁止令]] *[[ホームステッド法]]
{{デフォルトソート:こんてんえいねんしさいほう}} [[Category:日本の格式]] [[Category:日本の土地制度史]] [[Category:奈良時代の政治]] [[Category:8世紀の法]] [[Category:743年の日本]] [[Category:日本の農業関連法規]] [[Category:聖武天皇]]

'''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Konden Einen Shizai Law]] }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[sn:Konden Einen Shizai Law]] [[en:Konden Einen Shizai Law]] [[ja:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:日本の格式
revision.text:
'''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[sn:Konden Einen Shizai Law]] [[en:Konden Einen Shizai Law]] [[ja:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[Category:743年の日本]] [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:奈良時代の政治]] [[Category:日本の土地制度史]] [[Category:日本の格式]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove invalid enwiki interwiki links
revision.text:
'''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[sn:Konden Einen Shizai Law]] [[ja:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[Category:743年の日本]] [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:奈良時代の政治]] [[Category:日本の土地制度史]] [[Category:日本の格式]] [[Category:日本の農業関連法規]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]]

However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). revision.comment: Merge [[Category:日本の土地制度史]] into [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] via bot
revision.text:
'''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[sn:Konden Einen Shizai Law]] [[ja:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:日本の農業関連法規]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]]

''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[sn:Konden Einen Shizai Law]] [[ja:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[Category:paes with no wikidata]] [[Category:paes with no wikidata]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[sn:Konden Einen Shizai Law]] [[sn:E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[Category:paes with no wikidata]] [[Category:paes with no wikidata]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q4389478]] Wikidata name: Konden Einen Shizai Law [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:18, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[sn:Konden Einen Shizai Law]] [[sn:E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]]

'''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.

This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{wikidata link|Q4389478}}

However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4389478]] Wikidata name: Konden Einen Shizai Law [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:18, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{wikidata link|Q4389478}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4389478]] Wikidata name: Konden Einen Shizai Law [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:18, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{wikidata link|Q4389478}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne.
