[[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4389478]] Wikidata name: Konden Einen Shizai Law [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:18, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{wikidata link|Q4389478}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{wikidata link|Q4389478}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4389478]] Wikidata name: Konden Einen Shizai Law [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:18, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{wikidata link|Q4389478}} [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{wikidata link|Q4389478}} [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q4389478]] Wikidata name: Konden Einen Shizai Law [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:18, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{wikidata link|Q4389478}} [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{wikidata link|Q4389478}} [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] '''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{wikidata link|Q4389478}} [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]][[ja:墾田永年私財法]][[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]][[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]][[zh:墾田永年私財法]] '''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law''' (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Hō'') was an imperial edict (a decree issued in the name of the Emperor) promulgated on May 27, 743 (June 23, 743 in the Gregorian calendar) during the reign of Emperor Shōmu in the mid-Nara period. This law recognized the perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated land (''konden''). '''Also known as:''' * ''Perpetual Private Property Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私財法, ''Konden Eisei Shizai Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Law for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永世私有法, ''Konden Eisei Shiyū Hō'' [1]) * ''Perpetual Private Ownership Edict for Cultivated Land'' (墾田永代私有令, ''Konden Eitai Shiyū Rei'' [2]) This law laid the foundation for the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system [3]. ---- === Original Text === '''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku (類聚三代格)''' The original text of the ''Perpetual Private Ownership of Newly Cultivated Land Law'' is recorded in Volume 15 of the ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku''.
'''Imperial Edict:''' Based on the law from the 7th year of Yōro (723), cultivated land was subject to state reclamation after a set period. This led to farmers losing motivation, causing cultivated lands to become desolate again. Henceforth, newly cultivated lands shall be treated as private property in perpetuity, without limitations tied to three generations or personal tenure. During a provincial governor's term, the handling of cultivated land shall follow previous regulations. However, anyone wishing to cultivate new land must first apply to the provincial government. Occupying or claiming land that causes public inconvenience is prohibited. If land remains uncultivated for three years after permission is granted, others may apply to cultivate it.
''Issued: May 27, 743 (Tenpyō 15)''
---- === Background === Following the establishment of the Ritsuryō system and the ''Handen Shūju'' (equal field) system, by the early 8th century, the increasing population led to a shortage of land for allocation. The government sought to encourage new cultivation through policies like the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'', 723), which allowed land to remain private for up to three generations. However, land often fell into disuse after the expiration period [4]. Additionally, Japan was recovering from the Great Smallpox Epidemic (735-737), which severely impacted the population and economy. The 743 law aimed to support societal recovery by allowing perpetual private ownership of newly cultivated lands [5]. ---- === Content === The law abolished the reclamation period established by the ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'', allowing for perpetual private ownership. Key restrictions included: * Applied only to newly cultivated lands; existing cultivated lands remained under the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6]. * Private ownership limits were based on Rank, ranging from 10 to 500 ''chō'' [6]. * Required approval from provincial governors [7]. * Cultivation had to begin within three years of approval [7]. * Land claims should not hinder the general populace [8]. ---- === Interruption of the Law === During the tenure of Dōkyō as ''Daijō Daijin Zenji'' under Empress Shōtoku, a ''Daijōkanpu'' (imperial decree) issued on March 6, 765, restricted private ownership of cultivated land, allowing it primarily for temples and small-scale farmers [9]. However, after Dōkyō's fall following Empress Shōtoku's death, Emperor Kōnin reinstated private ownership on October 14, 772 [9]. ---- === Impact === The recognition of private ownership spurred local elites and religious institutions to expand landholdings, fostering the development of the ''shōen'' (private estate) system and undermining the ''Handen Shūju'' system [6][8]. These estates are known as ''jikondei-kei shōen'' or ''konden-kei shōen'' [10]. ---- === References === [1] Digital Daijisen [2] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [3] Mypedia Encyclopedia [4] Sekai Daihyakka (World Encyclopedia) [5] Farris, William Wayne. ''Population, Disease, and Land in Early Japan, 645-900'', Harvard University Asia Center (1985). [6] Nipponica (Japanese Encyclopedia) [7] Mypedia Encyclopedia [8] Obunsha Japanese History Encyclopedia [9] ''Ruisan Sandai Kyaku'', Volume 15 [10] Digital Daijisen = == See Also === * Ritsuryō System * ''Three Generations Private Ownership Law'' (''Sansei Isshin Hō'') * ''Jōri'' System * Cultivation Prohibition Orders * Homestead Act (U.S. equivalent) {{translated page|ja|墾田永年私財法|version=102390797|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[fr:Loi sur la propri%C3%A9t%C3%A9 des terres cultiv%C3%A9es en permanence]] [[uk:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%BE %D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%BD%D1%83 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D1%96%D1%8E %D1%86%D1%96%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D0%B8]] [[ru:%D0%97%D0%B0%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD %D0%BE %D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B9 %D0%BF%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%B7%D0%B0%D1%86%D0%B8%D0%B8 %D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%8B]] [[zh:%E5%A2%BE%E7%94%B0%E6%B0%B8%E5%B9%B4%E7%A7%81%E8%B2%A1%E6%B3%95]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] [[fr:Loi sur la propriété des terres cultivées en permanence]] [[ja:墾田永年私財法]] [[ru:Закон о вечной приватизации целины]] [[uk:Закон про довічну приватизацію цілини]] [[zh:墾田永年私財法]] {{wikidata link|Q4389478}} [[Category:8th century in law]] [[Category:Agricultural Legislation in Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Emperor Shōmu]] [[Category:History of Land Administration in Japan]] [[Category:Japan in 743]] [[Category:Kyaku and Shiki of Japan]] [[Category:Politics of the Nara Period]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]
